name: Ebro Green Corridor Trail
name_original: El Bocal a Tudela — circular en el Camino Natural del Ebro
description: This difficult 10.4-mile (16.7 km) jogging route follows the Camino Natural del Ebro from El Bocal to Tudela, featuring riverside scenery an
region_intro: Jogging routes around Fontellas offer diverse terrain in the Navarre region of Spain. The area is characterized by the Ebro River and the Canal Imperial de Aragón, providing flat, accessible paths. Nearby, the Bardenas Reales Natural Park presents a semi-desert landscape with unique clay, chalk, and sandstone formations, including canyons and isolated hills. This combination allows for a range of running experiences, from gentle riverside jogs to more challenging trails through distinctive geological features. Best jogging routes around Fontellas The most popular jogging route is Ebro Green Corridor, a 5.3 miles (8.5 km) trail that takes about 55 minutes to complete, following the scenic Ebro River. Another top favourite among local runners is Ebro Green Corridor: Tudela - El Bocal Trail, a moderate 8.5 miles (13.7 km) path. This route offers a pleasant run along the waterway, connecting Tudela to El Bocal. Local runners also love the Camino Natural del Ebro – Ebro Nature Trail loop from Tudela de Navarra, a 7.0 miles (11.2 km) trail leading through natural riverside areas, often completed in about 1 hour 10 minutes. Jogging around Fontellas is defined by serene riverside paths, the linear Canal Imperial de Aragón, and the unique semi-desert landscapes of Bardenas Reales. The network offers options for various ability levels, from easy, flat runs to more demanding trails. The routes in Fontellas are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 5.0 stars from over 2 reviews. More than 600 runners have used komoot to explore Fontellas's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many running routes are available around Fontellas?
A: There are nearly 100 dedicated running routes around Fontellas, offering a wide variety of experiences. These include 9 easy routes, 77 moderate routes, and 12 more challenging options, ensuring there's something for every runner.

Q: Are there any easy jogging routes suitable for beginners or a relaxed pace?
A: Yes, Fontellas offers 9 easy jogging routes perfect for beginners or those seeking a relaxed run. Many of these follow the flat, accessible paths alongside the Ebro River or the Canal Imperial de Aragón, providing gentle terrain and pleasant scenery.

Q: What kind of unique landscapes can I expect to see while running near Fontellas?
A: Fontellas is uniquely positioned near the dramatic, semi-desert landscapes of &lt;strong&gt;Bardenas Reales Natural Park&lt;/strong&gt;, just a short drive away. This area features striking clay, chalk, and sandstone formations, offering an otherworldly backdrop for trail running. You can also enjoy serene paths along the Ebro River and the Canal Imperial de Aragón, providing a contrast with agricultural scenery.

Q: Are there any circular running routes around Fontellas?
A: Yes, you can find circular running routes. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/17544796" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Camino Natural del Ebro – Ebro Nature Trail loop from Tudela de Navarra&lt;/a&gt; is a popular 7.0-mile (11.2 km) option that allows you to start and finish in the same location while exploring natural riverside areas.

Q: What are some scenic running routes with viewpoints or natural landmarks?
A: Many routes along the Ebro River offer scenic views of the waterway and surrounding nature. For unique natural landmarks, venturing towards &lt;strong&gt;Bardenas Reales Natural Park&lt;/strong&gt; will provide spectacular formations like Castildetierra and Pisquerra, though these might require a short drive to access the park's trails. The paths along the Canal Imperial de Aragón also offer picturesque agricultural vistas.

Q: Can I bring my dog on the running trails in Fontellas?
A: Generally, many outdoor trails in Spain, especially those in natural parks or along canals and rivers, are dog-friendly, provided your dog is kept on a leash and you clean up after them. Always check specific park regulations if you plan to run in protected areas like Bardenas Reales. The riverside and canal paths are typically suitable for running with dogs.

Q: Are there any family-friendly jogging routes in the area?
A: Yes, the flatter and more accessible routes along the Ebro River and the Canal Imperial de Aragón are excellent for families. These paths often have minimal elevation gain and provide a safe environment for children to join in, whether jogging or cycling alongside. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1144302323/ebro-green-corridor" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ebro Green Corridor&lt;/a&gt; is a good example of a relatively flat and enjoyable route.

Q: What can I see along the running routes near Fontellas?
A: Many routes offer views of the Ebro River and the Canal Imperial de Aragón. If you run closer to Tudela, you might pass historical sites like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4766416" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cathedral of Santa María de Tudela&lt;/a&gt; or the vibrant &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5784182" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Plaza de los Fueros&lt;/a&gt;. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1145744280/ebro-green-corridor-tudela-el-bocal-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ebro Green Corridor: Tudela - El Bocal Trail&lt;/a&gt;, for instance, connects Tudela to El Bocal, where you can see the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1389734" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Bocal Dam&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Is there parking available near the running trailheads?
A: Yes, for many of the popular routes, especially those starting or passing through towns like Tudela or Fontellas, you will find public parking options. For trails within natural parks like Bardenas Reales, designated parking areas are usually available at key access points. It's advisable to check specific route details on komoot for parking information related to your chosen trail.

Q: What do other runners enjoy most about jogging in Fontellas?
A: The running routes in Fontellas are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 5.0 stars. Runners often praise the diverse terrain, from serene riverside paths to the unique semi-desert landscapes, and the variety of options available for different ability levels.

Q: Are there any longer running routes for endurance training?
A: Absolutely. For those looking for longer runs, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1140620349/ebro-green-corridor-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ebro Green Corridor Trail&lt;/a&gt; offers a challenging 10.4-mile (16.7 km) route. Another option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38168832" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Old Murchante Station loop from Tudela de Navarra&lt;/a&gt;, which covers 8.6 miles (13.9 km) and is rated as moderate difficulty.

Q: What is the best time of year to go running in Fontellas?
A: Spring and autumn generally offer the most pleasant running conditions in Fontellas, with milder temperatures. Summer can be quite hot, especially in the semi-desert areas like Bardenas Reales, so early morning or late evening runs are recommended. Winter can be cool but often suitable for running, particularly on the less exposed riverside paths.
tips: The El Bocal Oak is an impressive tree declared a Natural Monument by the Government of Navarre. This majestic oak, located in the municipality of Fontellas, near Tudela, is over 500 years old and reaches a height of over 30 meters. It was likely planted during the initial construction of the Imperial Canal of Aragon.

The El Bocal Gate House is a historic structure that forms part of the Imperial Canal of Aragon. Built in the 16th century, this house is essential for controlling the flow of water from the El Bocal Dam. It was originally designed to regulate the water diverted from the Ebro River into the canal, facilitating irrigation and navigation.
The Ebro Nature Trail is a route that follows the course of the river. There are panoramic views and an opportunity to run with the tranquility of nature.

An emblematic place that offers an impressive landscape. It is a place that attracts visitors interested in nature and photography. The El Bocal Dam is located near the El Bocal aqueduct and plays a fundamental role in regulating water and supplying the area.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this jogging route?
faqs.answer: This circular jogging route, covering approximately 16.7 kilometers, is estimated to take around 1 hour and 45 minutes to complete. This duration is based on a steady jogging pace.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on the Camino Natural del Ebro between El Bocal and Tudela?
faqs.answer: The terrain is quite varied, offering a diverse jogging experience. You'll encounter dedicated paths (senda), streets (calle), tracks (vereda), and some roads (carretera). Sections run along the right bank of the Ebro River, featuring fluvial environments, ancient paths, and areas flanked by reeds (cañaverales) and dikes. The broader Ebro valley also presents flat, fertile agricultural soils.

faqs.question: What are the main points of interest along this route?
faqs.answer: The route offers several notable features. You'll pass by the historic area of El Bocal, home to the 16th-century El Bocal Gate House and the Palacio de Carlos V. Don't miss the majestic El Bocal Oak, a natural monument over 500 years old, and the impressive Bocal Dam. You'll also encounter the Sluice House, and of course, you'll be running on sections of the Camino Natural del Ebro itself.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access the trailhead at El Bocal?
faqs.answer: Parking is generally available in the El Bocal area, which serves as a common starting point for routes along this section of the Camino Natural del Ebro. Look for designated parking spots near the historical complex.

faqs.question: Is this jogging route suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: This specific 16.7 km circular route is rated as difficult for jogging. While other shorter or hiking-focused routes in the vicinity might be easier, the distance and varied terrain of this particular loop make it more challenging. Beginners might find it demanding, so consider your fitness level before attempting it.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to jog this trail?
faqs.answer: Spring and autumn are generally the best times to jog this trail. The weather is milder, making for more comfortable conditions. Summers can be very hot in the Ebro valley, and while winters are generally mild, occasional cold snaps or rain could make the experience less pleasant.

faqs.question: Are dogs allowed on this route?
faqs.answer: Generally, dogs are permitted on the Camino Natural del Ebro. However, it's always recommended to keep your dog on a leash, especially when passing through agricultural areas, near livestock, or in more populated sections. Always clean up after your pet.

faqs.question: Do I need any permits or pay entrance fees to use this trail?
faqs.answer: No, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required to access and jog along the Camino Natural del Ebro or the historical sites in El Bocal. It is a public access trail.

faqs.question: Does this route intersect with other well-known trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route utilizes significant portions of other established trails. You will be jogging along sections of the Corredor Verde del Ebro, the Camino natural de Ebro - Tramo central, and the Camino Jacobeo del Ebro.

faqs.question: Are there any cafes or restaurants near the trail, especially in El Bocal or Tudela?
faqs.answer: While El Bocal itself is more historical, the nearby city of Tudela offers a good selection of cafes and restaurants where you can refuel after your jog. You'll find options ranging from traditional Spanish cuisine to quick bites.

faqs.question: What kind of natural scenery and wildlife can I expect to see?
faqs.answer: The trail offers panoramic views and a peaceful connection with nature, primarily following the Ebro River. You can expect to see significant natural areas like riparian forests, known as "sotos," including Soto Alto and Soto de los Tetones. The landscape is a blend of riverside ecosystems, historical pathways, and distinct geological features. Keep an eye out for various bird species typical of riverine environments.
